food_subgroup: Seafood noodle dish — rice vermicelli with prawns

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for prawn bee hoon; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXM 'Pasta and noodles' is the closest defensible parent category for rice vermicelli noodle dishes; no specific prawn bee hoon code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for prawn bee hoon or equivalent rice vermicelli seafood dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of prawn bee hoon as served in a Singapore or Filipino café context, approximately 350 g, comprising rice vermicelli, whole prawns, vegetables, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Bee Hoon with Prawns — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for prawn rice vermicelli stir-fry
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists fried bee hoon and prawn dishes separately. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Fried Bee Hoon' (plain) and 'Stir-fried Prawns' entries, combined proportionally to reflect a typical prawn bee hoon dish (approximately 60% noodle base, 30% prawns, 10% vegetables and sauce). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked rice vermicelli (NDB 20065) and cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) used to cross-validate macros. Per-100g values represent the composite mixed dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli / rice noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn bee hoon as a composite dish. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice vermicelli / rice noodles (GI range 53–58, mean ~53, low category). The prawn and vegetable components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 63.7) / 100 ≈ 34.0, based on 350 g serving with 18.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing about 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and limit portion size to manage sodium intake, as a full serving can contribute substantially to daily sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli has a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~53) but a high glycemic load per full serving (~34) due to the large carbohydrate quantity in a standard plate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion or substitute with a higher-fibre noodle alternative to reduce the glycemic load, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Prawns contribute dietary cholesterol (~78 mg per 100 g) and the dish carries moderate sodium; however, saturated fat is relatively low at 0.7 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the cholesterol contribution from prawns and the sodium from soy-based sauces, and should prefer steamed or lightly stir-fried preparation with minimal added oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Bee Hoon (also known as Prawn Bihun or Prawn Rice Vermicelli) is a popular noodle dish found across Singapore, the Philippines, and Southeast Asia, featuring thin, translucent rice vermicelli noodles stir-fried or served in broth with whole prawns (shrimp). The dish is typically prepared by stir-frying pre-soaked rice vermicelli with garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onions, then adding fresh or frozen prawns cooked until pink and tender. Bean sprouts and egg are common additions. The texture is silky and light, with the noodles absorbing the savoury umami flavours of the sauce and the natural sweetness of the prawns. In the Filipino context, this dish is a variant of pancit bihon, a traditional noodle dish associated with celebrations and longevity. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a hawker or café meal. Nutritionally, prawn bee hoon provides a moderate energy density of approximately 138 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 8.5 g per 100 g from the prawns, which are a lean source of complete protein and supply vitamin B12, phosphorus, and zinc. The rice vermicelli base provides readily digestible carbohydrates with a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~53). Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-suitable when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn bee hoon?
Is prawn bee hoon suitable for diabetics?
What is the glycemic index of bee hoon?
Is prawn bee hoon high in sodium?
What is the difference between bee hoon and bihun?
What nutrients are in prawn rice vermicelli?
Can I eat prawn bee hoon if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Lucky Prawn Beehon' as a named dish exists; nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo fried bee hoon and stir-fried prawn entries with proportional composition modelling, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source; USDA SR Legacy reports trace selenium in shrimp but a reliable composite value cannot be derived without exact proportional weights. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed as negligible/trace in the source data for this dish type. 5. The food_name has been normalised to 'Prawn Bee Hoon' as the canonical English name; 'Lucky Prawn Beehon' is retained as an alias reflecting the field survey menu listing. 6. The cuisine is listed as Filipino per the input record; however, bee hoon dishes are also deeply embedded in Singaporean hawker culture, and the dish is treated as a Singaporean-context dish for multilingual naming purposes, hence Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are provided. 7. Glycemic load per serving: (53 × 18.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5 serving multiplier) / 100 = (53 × 63.7) / 100 ≈ 33.8, rounded to 34.0.

Applied multilingual naming for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Confirmed GI source is University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. Verified ontology match_type values are within allowed set (exact/closest_parent/none). Confirmed no zero core macros requiring justification.
Iteration 2: Self-scored draft. All 6 hard gates pass. All 8 Q criteria pass. Score 8/8. Decision: PASS. No further revision required.
